title: Replication: Beaudry and Portier (2014)
text: document replicates news shock identification (beaudryportier2014?). news shock identified shock () contemporaneous effect TFP (ii) maximizes long-run forecast error variance TFP 40-quarter horizon. approach follows mixed restriction strategy combining zero impact restriction long-run maximization criterion.

title: Replication: Beaudry and Portier (2014)
code: pkgload::load_all(here::here())  # recompiles changed C++ files and loads package  library(tidyverse) library(tictoc)  set_theme(ftheme_tidyMacro())

title: Replication: Beaudry and Portier (2014)
code: load(here::here('data', 'BeaudryPortier2014.rda'))  # Variables: log TFP, log SP500, log Consumption, log Hours y     <- BeaudryPortier2014 |> as.matrix() T_obs <- nrow(y) N     <- ncol(y)

title: Replication: Beaudry and Portier (2014)
code: p <- 2 c <- 1  # Estimate VAR: y_t = c + A_1 y_{t-1} + ... + A_p y_{t-p} + e_t var_result <- fVAR(y, p = p, c = c)  # Variance-covariance matrix of reduced-form residuals Sigma <- var_result$sigma_full

title: Replication: Beaudry and Portier (2014)
text: news shock identified shock zero impact TFP maximises cumulative long-run response TFP 40-quarter horizon. Let Ψh\Psi_h denote hh-step Wold coefficient matrices SS lower Cholesky factor Σ\Sigma. search unit vector 𝐡\mathbf{h} structural impact vector : 𝐛=S(0𝐡/∥𝐡∥) \mathbf{b} = S \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ \mathbf{h}/\|\mathbf{h}\| \end{pmatrix} long-run effect TFP, ∑h=0He1⊤Ψh𝐛\sum_{h=0}^{H} e_1^\top \Psi_h \mathbf{b}, maximized.
code: horizon <- 40  # Wold IRFs by inverting (I - A(L)) wold <- fwoldIRF(var_result, horizon = horizon)  # Lower Cholesky factor of Sigma S <- t(chol(Sigma))  # Objective: minimise negative LR effect on variable 1 (TFP) # subject to zero impact (first element of h2 fixed to 0) lr_max <- function(theta, wold, S, var_idx) {   h2        <- c(0, theta / sqrt(sum(theta^2)))   lr_effect <- sum(sapply(1:dim(wold)[3], function(h) wold[var_idx, , h] %*% S %*% h2))   return(-lr_effect) }  set.seed(12345) h0     <- rep(1, N - 1)   # N=4 variables, so 3 free parameters result <- optim(   par     = h0,   fn      = lr_max,   wold    = wold,   S       = S,   var_idx = 1,   method  = "Nelder-Mead",   control = list(reltol = 1e-10, maxit = 1e6) )  h  <- result$par h2 <- c(0, h / sqrt(sum(h^2)))  # Structural IRFs struct_irf <- matrix(0, nrow = N, ncol = horizon + 1) for (hh in 1:(horizon + 1)) {   struct_irf[, hh] <- wold[,, hh] %*% S %*% h2 }

title: Replication: Beaudry and Portier (2014)
text: Figure 1: Impulse response news shock
code: varnames <- c("TFP", "SP500", "Consumption", "Hours")  irf_df <- as.data.frame(t(struct_irf)) colnames(irf_df) <- varnames irf_df$horizon <- 0:horizon  irf_df |>   pivot_longer(-horizon, names_to = "variable", values_to = "response") |>   mutate(variable = factor(variable, levels = varnames)) |>   ggplot(aes(x = horizon, y = response)) +   geom_line() +   geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey50") +   facet_wrap(~variable, ncol = 2, scales = "free_y") +   labs(x = "Horizon (quarters)", y = NULL)

title: Replication: Beaudry and Portier (2014)
text: Figure 2: IRF news shock 68% LR-Max bootstrap bands
code: tic() boot_max <- fbootstrapMaxCorrected(   y          = y,   var_result = var_result,   nboot1     = 2000,   nboot2     = 4000,   horizon    = horizon,   var_idx    = 1L,        # maximise long-run effect on TFP (variable 1)   prc        = 68,   cumulate   = integer(0) # no cumulation (levels data) ) toc() #> 0.776 sec elapsed varnames <- c("TFP", "SP500", "Consumption", "Hours")  irf_to_df <- function(mat, label) {   as.data.frame(t(mat)) |>     setNames(varnames) |>     mutate(horizon = 0:(ncol(mat) - 1), type = label) }  bind_rows(   irf_to_df(struct_irf,         "Point"),   irf_to_df(boot_max$upper,     "Upper"),   irf_to_df(boot_max$lower,     "Lower") ) |>   pivot_longer(-c(horizon, type), names_to = "variable", values_to = "response") |>   mutate(variable = factor(variable, levels = varnames)) |>   pivot_wider(names_from = type, values_from = response) |>   ggplot(aes(x = horizon)) +   geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = Lower, ymax = Upper), alpha = 0.2) +   geom_line(aes(y = Point)) +   geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey50") +   facet_wrap(~variable, ncol = 2, scales = "free_y") +   labs(x = "Horizon (quarters)", y = NULL,        caption = "Shaded region: 68% LR-Max bootstrap confidence band")

what: Bootstrap Confidence Bands: Uhlig Max-Share Identification
title: Replication: Beaudry and Portier (2014)
text: Figure 3: IRF news shock 68% Uhlig max-share bootstrap bands
code: tic() boot_uhlig <- fbootstrapUhlig(   y          = y,   var_result = var_result,   nboot      = 1000,   horizon    = horizon,   idx        = 1L,        # maximise FEV share of TFP (variable 1)   prc        = 68,   cumulate   = integer(0) ) toc() #> 0.161 sec elapsed # Point estimate via Uhlig identification h2_uhlig <- fuhlig_maxshare(wold, S, idx = 1L) # Fix sign: last-horizon non-structural response of TFP should be positive last_ns <- (wold[,, dim(wold)[3]] %*% S) if (sum(last_ns[1, ] * h2_uhlig) < 0) h2_uhlig <- -h2_uhlig  struct_irf_uhlig <- matrix(0, nrow = N, ncol = horizon + 1) for (hh in 1:(horizon + 1)) {   struct_irf_uhlig[, hh] <- wold[,, hh] %*% S %*% h2_uhlig }  bind_rows(   irf_to_df(struct_irf_uhlig,   "Point"),   irf_to_df(boot_uhlig$upper,   "Upper"),   irf_to_df(boot_uhlig$lower,   "Lower") ) |>   pivot_longer(-c(horizon, type), names_to = "variable", values_to = "response") |>   mutate(variable = factor(variable, levels = varnames)) |>   pivot_wider(names_from = type, values_from = response) |>   ggplot(aes(x = horizon)) +   geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = Lower, ymax = Upper), alpha = 0.2) +   geom_line(aes(y = Point)) +   geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey50") +   facet_wrap(~variable, ncol = 2, scales = "free_y") +   labs(x = "Horizon (quarters)", y = NULL,        caption = "Shaded region: 68% Uhlig max-share bootstrap confidence band")

title: Replication: Bloom (2009)
text: document replicates main empirical results (bloom2009?), identifies uncertainty shocks using Cholesky decomposition. uncertainty indicator — constructed 17 identified high-volatility events — ordered first contemporaneous effect variables unaffected within month. Reference: Bloom, N. (2009). impact uncertainty shocks. Econometrica, 77(3), 623–685.

title: Replication: Bloom (2009)
code: library(tidyverse) library(tidyMacro) library(tictoc)  set_theme(ftheme_tidyMacro())

title: Replication: Bloom (2009)
code: data("Bloom2009")  dates_vec <- Bloom2009 |> pull(Date) y         <- Bloom2009 |> select(-Date) |> as.matrix()  T <- nrow(y) N <- ncol(y)  var_names <- colnames(y) shockname <- "UNCERT" shock     <- match(shockname, var_names)

title: Replication: Bloom (2009)
code: p <- 12   # lag length used in the original paper c <- 1  var_bloom <- fVAR(y, p, c) sigma     <- var_bloom$sigma_full  # Cholesky factor (lower triangular) S <- t(chol(sigma))  # Wold IRFs horizon   <- 48 wold      <- fwoldIRF(var_bloom, horizon = horizon) point_irf <- fcholeskyIRF(wold, S)

title: Replication: Bloom (2009)
code: tic() bloom_chol <- fbootstrapChol(     y          = y,     var_result = var_bloom,     nboot      = 1000,     horizon    = horizon,     prc        = 68,     bootscheme = "residual" ) toc() #> 16.609 sec elapsed tic() bloom_corrected <- fbootstrapCholCorrected(     y          = y,     var_result = var_bloom,     nboot1     = 1000,     nboot2     = 2000,     horizon    = horizon,     prc        = 68,     bootscheme = "residual" ) toc() #> 39.265 sec elapsed

title: Replication: Bloom (2009)
code: tic() bloom_chol <- fbootstrapChol(     y          = y,     var_result = var_bloom,     nboot      = 1000,     horizon    = horizon,     prc        = 68,     bootscheme = "residual" ) toc() #> 16.609 sec elapsed

title: Replication: Bloom (2009)
code: tic() bloom_corrected <- fbootstrapCholCorrected(     y          = y,     var_result = var_bloom,     nboot1     = 1000,     nboot2     = 2000,     horizon    = horizon,     prc        = 68,     bootscheme = "residual" ) toc() #> 39.265 sec elapsed

title: Replication: Bloom (2009)
text: Figure 1: IRFs uncertainty shock — standard bootstrap (68% confidence bands) Figure 2: IRFs uncertainty shock — bias-corrected bootstrap (68% confidence bands)
code: fplotirf_chol(     point_irf,     bloom_chol$upper,     bloom_chol$lower,     shock    = shock,     varnames = var_names,     prc      = 68,     facet_ncol = 4 ) fplotirf_chol(     point_irf,     bloom_corrected$upper,     bloom_corrected$lower,     shock    = shock,     varnames = var_names,     prc      = 68,     facet_ncol = 4 )

title: Replication: Bloom (2009)
text: Figure 3: Forecast error variance decomposition: share explained uncertainty shock
code: vardec <- fevd_chol(point_irf, shock = shock)  fplot_vardec(     fevd       = vardec$fevd,     varnames   = var_names,     shocknames = shockname )

title: Replication: Bloom (2009)
text: Figure 4: Historical decomposition: contribution uncertainty shock
code: # Exclude the shock variable itself from the response variables series <- setdiff(seq_len(N), shock)  histdec_list <- setNames(     lapply(series, function(i) fhistdec(y, var_bloom, S, i)$histdec),     colnames(y)[series] )  fplot_histdec(     histdec_list = histdec_list,     shock        = shock,     shockname    = shockname,     dates        = dates_vec,     p            = p,     facet_ncol   = 2 ) +     scale_x_date(date_breaks = "7 years", date_labels = "%Y")

title: Replication: Galí (1999)
text: document replicates main empirical results Galí (1999), identifies technology shocks using Blanchard-Quah (BQ) long-run restriction: technology shocks can permanent effect labor productivity. VAR estimated US data first differences, level responses recovered cumulating IRFs.

title: Replication: Galí (1999)
code: library(tidyverse) library(tidyMacro) library(tictoc)  set_theme(ftheme_tidyMacro())

title: Replication: Galí (1999)
code: data("Gali1999")  y <- Gali1999 |> select(Productivity, Hours) |> as.matrix() dates <- Gali1999 |> pull(Date)  T <- nrow(y) N <- ncol(y)

title: Replication: Galí (1999)
code: # Lag length selection via AIC p <- fAICBIC(y, pmax = 4, c = 1)$aic c <- 1  # Estimate VAR: y_t = c + A_1 y_{t-1} + ... + A_p y_{t-p} + e_t var_result <- fVAR(y, p = p, c = c)  # Variance-covariance matrix of reduced-form residuals Sigma <- var_result$sigma_full

title: Replication: Galí (1999)
text: Galí (1999) assumes technology shocks permanent effect labour productivity. imposes lower-triangular structure long-run multiplier matrix C1KC_1 K, identified via: C1=∑h=0HΨh,D1=chol(C1ΣC1′)⊤,K=C1−1D1 C_1 = \sum_{h=0}^{H} \Psi_h, \quad D_1 = \text{chol}(C_1 \Sigma C_1')^\top, \quad K = C_1^{-1} D_1
code: horizon <- 40 wold <- fwoldIRF(var_result, horizon = horizon)  # Long-run multiplier matrix C1 <- apply(wold, c(1, 2), sum)  # Lower Cholesky of long-run covariance D1 <- t(chol(C1 %*% Sigma %*% t(C1)))  # Structural impact matrix K <- solve(C1, D1)  # BQ IRFs (in first differences) irf_bq <- fbqIRF(wold, K)

title: Replication: Galí (1999)
code: tic() boot_bq <- fbootstrapBQ(   y = y,   var_result = var_result,   nboot = 1000,   horizon = 40,   prc = 68,   bootscheme = "residual",   cumulate = c(1, 2) ) toc() #> 0.066 sec elapsed

title: Replication: Galí (1999)
text: Level responses recovered cumulating first-differenced IRFs. first shock technology shock; second non-technology shock. Figure 1: Impulse response functions technology non-technology shocks (68% confidence bands)
code: source(here::here('R', 'fplotirf_bq.R')) fplotirf_bq(   point = irf_bq,   upper = boot_bq$upper,   lower = boot_bq$lower,   varnames = c("LABPROD", "HOURS"),   shocknames = c("Shock:Technology", "Shock:Non-Technology"),   cumulate = c(1, 2),   shocks = c(1, 2),   prc = 68 )

title: Replication: Galí (1999)
text: Figure 2: Forecast error variance decomposition
code: # Cumulate BQ IRF along horizon (dim 3) to get level responses irf_cumu <- irf_bq for (h in 2:dim(irf_bq)[3]) {   irf_cumu[,, h] <- irf_cumu[,, h] + irf_cumu[,, h - 1] }  # FEVD — reuses Cholesky FEVD function (same maths) fevd <- fevd_chol(irf_cumu, shock = 0)$fevd fevd <- fevd[, c(2, 1), ] # optional, for vis purposes  fplot_vardec(   fevd = fevd,   varnames = c("LABPROD", "HOURS"),   shocknames = c("Technology", "Non-Technology") ) +   scale_fill_manual(values = tidyMacro_colors[1:2])

title: Replication: Galí (1999)
text: Figure 3: Historical decomposition: contribution technology shocks
code: histdec_all <- vector("list", N) ystar_all <- matrix(0, nrow = T - p, ncol = N)  for (series in seq_len(N)) {   res <- fhistdec(y, var_result, K, series)   histdec_all[[series]] <- res$histdec   ystar_all[, series] <- res$ystar }  names(histdec_all) <- c("LABPROD", "HOURS")  fplot_histdec(   histdec_list = histdec_all,   shock = 1,   shockname = "Technology",   dates = dates,   p = p,   facet_ncol = 1 ) +   scale_x_date(date_breaks = "6 years", date_labels = "%Y")

title: Replication: Galí (1999)
text: Following Galí (1999), decompose unconditional correlation hours productivity shock-specific conditional correlations. conditional correlation shock ii : ρi=∑h=0HΨ1i,hΨ2i,h(∑h=0HΨ1i,h2)(∑h=0HΨ2i,h2) \rho_i = \frac{\sum_{h=0}^{H} \Psi_{1i,h} \, \Psi_{2i,h}}{\sqrt{\left(\sum_{h=0}^{H} \Psi_{1i,h}^2\right)\left(\sum_{h=0}^{H} \Psi_{2i,h}^2\right)}} unconditional correlation hours productivity small, odds standard RBC models. However, conditional correlation driven technology shock negative: positive technology shock reduces hours worked. non-technology shock yields positive conditional correlation — pattern RBC theory incorrectly attributed technology.
code:
# Dimensions of irf_bq: [variables, shocks, horizon] N_shocks <- dim(irf_bq)[2]  # Numerator: for each shock i, sum of cross-products of IRFs of var 1 and var 2 numerator <- numeric(N_shocks) for (i in 1:N_shocks) {   temp         <- irf_bq[1, i, ] * irf_bq[2, i, ]   numerator[i] <- sum(temp) }  # Denominator: sqrt of product of sum-of-squares for each shock irf_squared <- irf_bq^2 denominator <- numeric(N_shocks) for (i in 1:N_shocks) {   v1             <- sum(irf_squared[1, i, ])   v2             <- sum(irf_squared[2, i, ])   denominator[i] <- sqrt(v1 * v2) }  # Conditional correlations (one per shock) cond_corr <- numerator / denominator  # Unconditional correlations uncond_corr <- cor(y)  cat("Unconditional correlation (Productivity, Hours):", round(uncond_corr[1, 2], 3), "
") #> Unconditional correlation (Productivity, Hours): -0.203 cat("Conditional correlation | Technology shock:     ", round(cond_corr[1], 3), "
") #> Conditional correlation | Technology shock:      -0.901 cat("Conditional correlation | Non-Technology shock: ", round(cond_corr[2], 3), "
") #> Conditional correlation | Non-Technology shock:  0.733

title: Replication: Kaenzig (2021)
text: document replicates main empirical results Känzig (2021) , identifies oil supply news shocks using high-frequency external instrument (proxy SVAR). instrument captures exogenous variation oil supply driven OPEC production decisions, measured oil futures price changes narrow window around OPEC announcements.

title: Replication: Kaenzig (2021)
code: library(tidyverse) library(tidyMacro) library(tictoc)  set_theme(ftheme_tidyMacro())

title: Replication: Kaenzig (2021)
code: data("Kaenzig2021") finaldata <- Kaenzig2021 |>     select(Oil_Price, World_Oil_Prod, World_Oil_Inven, World_IP, US_IP, US_CPI) |>     as.matrix()  iv_oil <- Kaenzig2021 |>     select(iv_kanzig_final) |>     drop_na() |>     as.matrix()  T <- nrow(finaldata) N <- ncol(finaldata)  varnames  <- c("Oil Price", "World Oil Prod.", "World Oil Inven.",                "World IP", "US IP", "US CPI") shockname <- "Oil Shock"

title: Replication: Kaenzig (2021)
code: p <- 12 c <- 1  var_result <- fVAR(finaldata, p, c) residuals  <- var_result$residuals sigma_full <- var_result$sigma_full  # Wold IRFs hor  <- 48 wold <- fwoldIRF(var_result, horizon = hor)

title: Replication: Kaenzig (2021)
code: tic() result_mbb <- fbootstrapIV_mbb(     y          = finaldata,     var_result = var_result,     Z          = iv_oil,     nboot      = 1000,     blocksize  = 0,          # auto block size     adjustZ    = c(1, 417),     adjustu    = c(100, 516),     policyvar  = 1,     horizon    = hor,     prc        = 90 ) #> Using 3 thread(s) for parallel bootstrap computation... toc() #> 6.996 sec elapsed

title: Replication: Kaenzig (2021)
text: Figure 1: IRFs oil supply news shock — MBB bootstrap (90% confidence bands)
code: fplotirf_iv(     point     = result_mbb$meanirf * 10,     upper     = result_mbb$upper * 10,     lower     = result_mbb$lower * 10,     varnames  = varnames,     shockname = shockname,     prc       = 90,     facet_ncol = 3 )

title: Replication: Kaenzig (2021)
text: proxy SVAR identified manually following two-stage IV approach. instrument ZtZ_t regressed reduced-form residual policy variable (oil price) obtain fitted values ûp,t\hat{u}_{p,t}, used identify structural impact vector ss.
code: # Align residuals and instrument to overlapping sample u_p     <- residuals[, 1] u_p_fin <- u_p[(length(u_p) - length(iv_oil) + 1):length(u_p)] |> as.matrix() u_q_fin <- residuals[     (nrow(residuals) - length(iv_oil) + 1):nrow(residuals),     2:ncol(residuals) ] |> as.matrix()  u   <- residuals[(nrow(residuals) - length(iv_oil) + 1):nrow(residuals), ] T1  <- nrow(u) sigma <- (t(u) %*% u) / (T1 - 1 - p - N * p) S     <- t(chol(sigma))  # Stage 1: regress u_p on instrument ols1 <- fOLS(y = u_p_fin, X = iv_oil, c = 0) uhat <- ols1$fitted_partial  # Stage 2: regress other residuals on uhat to get s_q / s_p sq_sp <- solve(t(uhat) %*% uhat) %*% t(uhat) %*% u_q_fin  # Structural impact vector (normalised to unit oil price response) s <- c(1, sq_sp[1], sq_sp[2], sq_sp[3], sq_sp[4], sq_sp[5])  # Structural IRFs ivirf <- matrix(0, nrow = N, ncol = hor + 1) for (h in 1:(hor + 1)) {     ivirf[, h] <- wold[, , h] %*% s }

title: Replication: Kaenzig (2021)
text: Figure 2: Forecast error variance decomposition: share explained oil supply news shock
code: fevd_result <- fevd_iv(s, S, wold, N, hor, sigma, u, T1 = T1, p = p)  fplot_vardec(     fevd       = fevd_result$fevd_iv,     varnames   = varnames,     shocknames = shockname )

title: Determine Optimal Lag Order for VAR Model Using Information Criteria — fAICBIC

title: Determine Optimal Lag Order for VAR Model Using Information Criteria — fAICBIC

title: Determine Optimal Lag Order for VAR Model Using Information Criteria — fAICBIC
text: candidate lag order p residual covariance Sigma = (e'e)/T computed three criteria evaluated : AIC = log(det(Sigma)) + 2 * (p*N^2 + N + M) / T BIC = log(det(Sigma)) + log(T) * (p*N^2 + N + M) / T HQ  = log(det(Sigma)) + 2 * log(log(T)) * (p*N^2 + N + M) / T M number exogenous variables.

code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ set.seed(123) y      <- matrix(rnorm(200), ncol = 2) result <- fAICBIC(y, pmax = 10, c = 1) result$aic result$bic } # }

title: Print SVAR Identification Steps — fSVAR_steps
text: Prints numbered guide proxy-SVAR identification invertibility-testing procedure R console.

title: Print SVAR Identification Steps — fSVAR_steps
code: fSVAR_steps()

title: Print SVAR Identification Steps — fSVAR_steps
text: Called side-effect printing console.   Returns NULL invisibly.

title: Print SVAR Identification Steps — fSVAR_steps
text: Step 1: Estimate VAR(p) extract residuals. Step 2: Regress instrument current residuals plus f leads; save fitted value. Step 3: F-test whether lead coefficients jointly zero. Rejection implies shock non-invertible. Step 4: invertible, apply standard Stock-Watson IV procedure obtain IRFs unit-variance shocks. Step 5: non-invertible, test recoverability via Ljung-Box test fitted instrument series. Step 6: recoverable (null autocorrelation rejected), repeat IV procedure adding residual leads first-stage regression. Step 7: recoverability rejected, SVAR analysis instrument feasible.

title: Print SVAR Identification Steps — fSVAR_steps
code: fSVAR_steps() #> Step 1: Estimate the VAR(p) and extract the residuals. #> Step 2: Regress the instrument on current residuals plus f leads. Save the fitted value. #> Step 3: F-test that lead coefficients are jointly zero. Rejection implies non-invertibility. #> Step 4: If invertible, apply the Stock-Watson IV procedure for IRFs and unit-variance shocks. #> Step 5: If non-invertible, test recoverability via the Ljung-Box test on the fitted instrument. #> Step 6: If recoverable, redo the IV procedure adding residual leads to the first-stage regression. #> Step 7: If recoverability is rejected, SVAR analysis with this instrument is not feasible.

title: Vector Autoregression (VAR) Model Estimation — fVAR

title: Bootstrap Blanchard-Quah Long-Run Identified Impulse Response Functions — fbootstrapBQ
text: Implements residual wild bootstrap BQ long-run identified VARs. replication full BQ identification re-computed: \(C_1 = \sum_h \Psi_h\), \(D_1 = \text{chol}(C_1 \Sigma C_1')^\top\), \(K = C_1^{-1} D_1\). Selected variables cumulated VAR estimated differences.

title: Bootstrap Blanchard-Quah Long-Run Identified Impulse Response Functions — fbootstrapBQ
text: Blanchard, O. J., & Quah, D. (1989). dynamic effects aggregate demand supply disturbances. American Economic Review, 79(4), 655–673. Gali, J. (1999). Technology, employment, business cycle: technology shocks explain aggregate fluctuations? American Economic Review, 89(1), 249–271.

title: Bootstrap Blanchard-Quah Long-Run Identified Impulse Response Functions — fbootstrapBQ
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ var_result <- fVAR(y, p = 2, c = 1) wold       <- fwoldIRF(var_result, horizon = 40) Sigma      <- var_result$sigma_full C1         <- apply(wold, c(1, 2), sum) D1         <- t(chol(C1 %*% Sigma %*% t(C1))) K          <- solve(C1, D1) point_irf  <- fbqIRF(wold, K)  # Bootstrap with cumulation of both variables (VAR in first differences) boot <- fbootstrapBQ(y, var_result, nboot = 1000, horizon = 40,                      prc = 68, bootscheme = "residual",                      cumulate = c(1L, 2L)) } # }

title: Compute Blanchard-Quah (BQ) Impulse Response Functions — fbqIRF
text: wold Wold representation cube (N x N x horizon+1), wold[,,h] contains Wold IRF horizon h K N x N lower triangular Cholesky factor long-run covariance matrix used BQ identification scaling Optional numeric vector length 2. first element specifies variable index (1-based) used normalisation, second element specifies shock size. omitted normalisation applied.

title: Compute Blanchard-Quah (BQ) Impulse Response Functions — fbqIRF
text: cube (N x N x horizon+1) long-run identified impulse response   functions.

title: Compute Blanchard-Quah (BQ) Impulse Response Functions — fbqIRF
text: Computes structural impulse response functions Blanchard-Quah (1989) long-run identification scheme. horizon h structural IRF computed : $$IRF_h = \Psi_h \cdot K$$ \(\Psi_h\) Wold representation horizon h K lower triangular Cholesky factor long-run covariance matrix. scaling supplied entire cube divided \(IRF_0(\text{scaling}[1],\, \text{scaling}[1]) \;/\; \text{scaling}[2]\), normalising impact response selected variable scaling[2].

title: Compute Blanchard-Quah (BQ) Impulse Response Functions — fbqIRF

code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Estimate a VAR and compute Wold IRFs VAR  <- fVAR(y, p = 2, c = 1) wold <- fwoldIRF(VAR, horizon = 20)  # Obtain BQ long-run Cholesky factor K (from e.g. fBQ()) bqirf <- fbqIRF(wold, K)  # With normalisation: unit shock to variable 1 bqirf_norm <- fbqIRF(wold, K, scaling = c(1, 1)) } # }

Cholesky identification imposes recursive structure contemporaneous relationships variables, ordering variables determining causal structure.

code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Generate sample Wold representation N <- 3 horizon <- 20 wold <- array(rnorm(N * N * horizon), dim = c(N, N, horizon))  # Compute Cholesky factor Sigma <- matrix(c(1, 0.5, 0.3,                   0.5, 1, 0.4,                   0.3, 0.4, 1), 3, 3) S <- t(chol(Sigma))  # Lower triangular  # Compute Cholesky IRF chol_irf <- fcholeskyIRF(wold, S) } # }

title: Forecast Error Variance Decomposition for Cholesky-Identified VARs — fevd_chol
text: forecast error variance decomposition measures proportion h-step ahead forecast error variance variable attributable structural shock j. Cholesky-identified VARs: $$FEVD(,j,h) = \frac{\sum_{s=0}^{h} [\Psi_s P]_{ij}^2}{\sum_{k=1}^{N} \sum_{s=0}^{h} [\Psi_s P]_{ik}^2}$$

title: Forecast Error Variance Decomposition for Cholesky-Identified VARs — fevd_chol
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Estimate VAR var_result <- fVAR(y, p = 2, c = 1) wold <- fwoldIRF(var_result, horizon = 20) S <- t(chol(var_result$sigma_full)) chol_irf <- fcholeskyIRF(wold, S)  # Full decomposition (all shocks) — returns 3D cube vardec <- fevd_chol(chol_irf)  # Single shock decomposition — returns N x horizon matrix shock <- match("UNCERT", colnames(y)) vardec <- fevd_chol(chol_irf, shock = shock) } # }

text: s N x 1 vector structural impact coefficients S N x N lower triangular Cholesky factor residual covariance matrix wold N x N x (hor+1) cube Wold impulse response functions N Integer number variables VAR system hor Integer maximum forecast horizon sigma N x N residual covariance matrix u (T-p) x N matrix VAR residuals T1 Integer effective sample size (T-p) p Integer lag order VAR model

title: Forecast Error Variance Decomposition for IV-Identified Shocks — fevd_iv
text: list containing:   - scaler: N x 1 vector scaling factors unit variance normalization   - ivirf_scaled: N x (hor+1) matrix unit variance impulse responses   - oil_news_unit_var: 1 x T1 vector unit variance shock series   - check_uv: Scalar variance check (close 1)   - denom: N x (hor+1) matrix total forecast error variances   - fevd_iv: N x (hor+1) matrix FEVD shares (0 1)

title: Forecast Error Variance Decomposition for IV-Identified Shocks — fevd_iv
text: FEVD measures proportion h-step ahead forecast error variance variable attributable identified structural shock. function performs following steps: 1. Normalizes structural shock unit variance 2. Computes unit variance impulse responses 3. Recovers unit variance shock series 4. Computes total forecast error variance horizon 5. Computes variance due identified shock 6. Calculates FEVD ratio FEVD values proportions 0 1, higher values indicate identified shock explains larger share forecast error variance.

title: Forecast Error Variance Decomposition for IV-Identified Shocks — fevd_iv
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Estimate VAR and compute Wold IRF var_result <- fVAR(y, p = 2, c = 1) wold <- fwoldIRF(var_result, horizon = 20)  # IV identification s <- c(1, 0.5, 0.3) sigma <- var_result$sigma_full S <- t(chol(sigma))  # Compute FEVD result <- fevd_iv(s, S, wold, N = 3, hor = 20,                   sigma = sigma, u = var_result$residuals,                   T1 = nrow(var_result$residuals), p = 2)  # Plot FEVD for first variable plot(result$fevd_iv[1, ], type = "l", ylim = c(0, 1),      main = "FEVD: Share of Variance Explained by Shock",      xlab = "Horizon", ylab = "Share") } # }

what: Historical Decomposition of a VAR Variable — fhistdec
title: Historical Decomposition of a VAR Variable — fhistdec
text: Decomposes chosen variable's realisation contributions structural shock, identified via impact matrix K. Supports Cholesky (lower-triangular K) Blanchard-Quah (general K) identification. Mirrors MATLAB hist_decmp(y, beta, residuals, c, p, K, series).

title: Historical Decomposition of a VAR Variable — fhistdec
code: fhistdec(y, fVAR, K, series)

title: Historical Decomposition of a VAR Variable — fhistdec
text: y TxN numeric matrix original (undemeaned) data. fVAR List returned fVAR(), containing minimum beta, residuals, sigma_full, p, c, n_exog. K NxN structural impact matrix. Cholesky identification pass t(chol(sigma_full)); BQ identification pass matrix returned solve(C1, D1). series Integer (1-indexed) selecting variable decompose.

title: Historical Decomposition of a VAR Variable — fhistdec
text: list two elements: histdec (T-p) x N numeric matrix. Column j     cumulative contribution structural shock j chosen     variable point time. ystar (T-p) numeric vector demeaned realisation     chosen variable (benchmark series plot).

title: Historical Decomposition of a VAR Variable — fhistdec
text: structural shocks recovered $$\varepsilon_t = K^{-1} u_t$$ \(u_t\) reduced-form residuals. structural MA representation built multiplying Wold matrix K: $$\Theta_h = \Psi_h K$$ contribution shock \(j\) variable \(\) time \(t\) inner product \(\Theta_{0:t}[,j]\) time-reversed structural shocks \(\varepsilon_{t:-1:0,j}\): $$HD(t,j) = \sum_{h=0}^{t} \Theta_h[,j]\, \varepsilon_{t-h,j}$$

title: Historical Decomposition of a VAR Variable — fhistdec
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Cholesky identification var_result <- fVAR(y, p = 12, c = 1) K_chol <- t(chol(var_result$sigma_full)) hd <- fhistdec(y, var_result, K_chol, series = 1)  # BQ identification wold  <- fwoldIRF(var_result, horizon = 40) C1    <- apply(wold, c(1, 2), sum) D1    <- t(chol(C1 %*% var_result$sigma_full %*% t(C1))) K_bq  <- solve(C1, D1) hd_bq <- fhistdec(y, var_result, K_bq, series = 1) } # }

title: Plot Blanchard-Quah Long-Run Identified Impulse Response Functions — fplotirf_bq
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ var_result <- fVAR(y, p = p, c = 1) wold       <- fwoldIRF(var_result, horizon = 40) Sigma      <- var_result$sigma_full C1         <- apply(wold, c(1, 2), sum) D1         <- t(chol(C1 %*% Sigma %*% t(C1))) K          <- solve(C1, D1) point_irf  <- fbqIRF(wold, K)  boot <- fbootstrapBQ(y, var_result, nboot = 1000, horizon = 40,                      prc = 68, bootscheme = "residual",                      cumulate = c(1, 2))  # Single shock — facets by variable only fplotirf_bq(   point      = point_irf,   upper      = boot$upper,   lower      = boot$lower,   varnames   = c("LABPROD", "HOURS"),   shocknames = "Technology",   cumulate   = c(1, 2),   shocks     = 1,   prc        = 68 )  # Multiple shocks — facets by shock x variable fplotirf_bq(   point      = point_irf,   upper      = boot$upper,   lower      = boot$lower,   varnames   = c("LABPROD", "HOURS"),   shocknames = c("Technology", "Non-Technology"),   cumulate   = c(1, 2),   shocks     = c(1, 2),   prc        = 68 ) } # }

title: Update ggplot2 Theme Defaults to tidyMacro Style — set_tidyMacro_theme
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ library(ggplot2) library(tidyMacro)  # Set tidyMacro as default theme for the session set_tidyMacro_theme()  # All subsequent plots will use tidyMacro styling ggplot(mtcars, aes(x = wt, y = mpg, color = factor(cyl))) +   geom_point()  # Revert to ggplot2 defaults theme_set(theme_gray()) } # }
